subject: Chemistry
title: Cadmium Yellow: Bright and Banned
description: A micro-lesson that makes Cadmium Yellow: Bright and Banned usable.

hook.text: Cadmium yellow is one of the brightest, most lightfast yellows ever made. It's also toxic and increasingly restricted.

buildup.text: Cadmium sulfide (CdS) produces intense yellow. Adding selenium shifts it to orange and red—a whole color family.

discovery.text: Monet, Van Gogh, and Matisse all used cadmium colors. Their paintings have kept their brilliance for over a century.

twist.text: Sweden proposed banning cadmium pigments in 2014. Artists protested—no synthetic alternative matches their color and stability.

climax.text: The EU exempted artist paints from the ban. Cadmium is too toxic for mass products but irreplaceable for fine art.

punchline.text: Toxic but irreplaceable. Some colors are too brilliant to have safe alternatives.

quiz.question: Why haven't cadmium pigments been fully replaced in artist paints?
options: No synthetic alternative matches their color intensity and lightfastness
Cadmium pigments are actually non-toxic in small amounts
Artists haven't been informed about the health risks
